coredata.dc:title: Modulating the physicochemical and biological properties of carbon dots synthesised from plastic waste for effective sensing of E. coli
coredata.dc:creator: Kumari, Manisha
Chaudhary, Savita
coredata.dc:description: The reliable means for the quick recognition of pathogenic bacteria for disease prevention has acquired a considerable significance among researchers for human wellbeing. The purpose of current study is to fabricate highly photo-luminescent and biocompatible carbon dots (CQDs) from single used plastic waste such as poly bags, cups and bottles for selective sensing of E. coli. The formed small sized spherical CQDs have possessed high water solubility with excellent photo stable nature. The detailed fluorescence emission studies of CQDs have illustrated the overview of different types of surface defects and emissive states in formed particles. In addition, the specific role of prepared CQDs with bio-compatible nature has been thoroughly checked on hemolysis of red blood cells by employing in-vitro blood compatibility assay. A combination of anticoagulant and thrombolytic activity of CQDs with anti-oxidative activities from reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation were found to be responsible for the biocompatibility of formed CQDs. The effective in vitro stability toward histidine, cysteine, bovine serum albumin (BSA), human serum albumin (HSA) and saline supported the biocompatibility of formed particles as a function of different concentrations of CQDs. The optimized fluorescence properties with high surface to volume ratio of CQDs have provided a better alternative for “turn-off” fluorescence detection of E. coil in aqueous media with limit of detection down to 108 CFU/mL estimated by using standard colony-counting method. The as prepared nanosensor provided easy fabrication with fast response, high sensitivity and selectivity in real water samples which might be necessary for detecting pathogenic agents. In addition, the current work has provided the large overview in blood compatibility and anti-oxidative potential of prepared CQDs and giving useful information for preparing blood–compatible nanoparticles with enhanced bacterial discrimination ability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Pathogen
E. coli
Blood compatibility
Thrombolytic activity
Turn-off sensor

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of CQDs from single used plastic waste
Characterization of fluorescent CQDs obtained from single used plastic waste
CQDs biocompatibility appraisals using in-vitro blood studies
In vitro stability assay for CQDs over blood components
Biocompatibility appraisal of CQDs by using in-vitro blood studies
Hemolysis, blood clotting, thrombolytic activity and anticoagulant assay
In vitro stability assay for CQDs over blood components
Analytical sensing ability of CQDs in real water samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Haryana State Council for Science and Technology
Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Tauc plot and Gaussian analysis of the emission spectra for (ai, bi) CQDP, (aii, bii) CQDc, and (aiii, biii) CQDb.

Effect of different concentrations of CQDs on (a) hemolysis ratio and (b) blood clotting index at λ =542 nm. The respective (c) anticoagulant activity and (d) in-vitro thrombolytic activity of prepared CQDs in presence of blood.

In-Vitro stability assay of synthesized CQDs (a-c) in the presence of different blood components (BSA, HSA, histidine, cysteine and saline) and variations in wavelength of CQDs (d-f) in the presence of different pH buffer solutions (pH = 5.7, 6.2, 7.0 and 7.5) for CQDP, CQDc, and CQDb.
Effect of different concentrations of CQDs on (a-c) anti-oxidative activities and (d-f) hydrogen peroxide free radical scavenging activities for CQDP, CQDc, and CQDb respectively.
Determination of different concentrations of E. coli by using colony formation method.
Variations in fluorescence emission profile of (a) CQDc, (b) CQDb and (c) CQDP in the presence of different concentrations of E. coli bacteria.
Stern-Volmer plot for linear correlation between fluorescence intensity and concentration of E. coli for (a) CQDc, (b) CQDb and (c) CQDP.
Recovery study of (a) CQDP (b) CQDc and (c) CQDb in presence of different concentrations of E. coli bacteria in four different real water samples.

head.ce:title: Modulating the physicochemical and biological properties of carbon dots synthesised from plastic waste for effective sensing of E. coli

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh 160014, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh 160014, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The reliable means for the quick recognition of pathogenic bacteria for disease prevention has acquired a considerable significance among researchers for human wellbeing. The purpose of current study is to fabricate highly photo-luminescent and biocompatible carbon dots (CQDs) from single used plastic waste such as poly bags, cups and bottles for selective sensing of E. coli. The formed small sized spherical CQDs have possessed high water solubility with excellent photo stable nature. The detailed fluorescence emission studies of CQDs have illustrated the overview of different types of surface defects and emissive states in formed particles. In addition, the specific role of prepared CQDs with bio-compatible nature has been thoroughly checked on hemolysis of red blood cells by employing in-vitro blood compatibility assay. A combination of anticoagulant and thrombolytic activity of CQDs with anti-oxidative activities from reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation were found to be responsible for the biocompatibility of formed CQDs. The effective in vitro stability toward histidine, cysteine, bovine serum albumin (BSA), human serum albumin (HSA) and saline supported the biocompatibility of formed particles as a function of different concentrations of CQDs. The optimized fluorescence properties with high surface to volume ratio of CQDs have provided a better alternative for “turn-off” fluorescence detection of E. coil in aqueous media with limit of detection down to 108 CFU/mL estimated by using standard colony-counting method. The as prepared nanosensor provided easy fabrication with fast response, high sensitivity and selectivity in real water samples which might be necessary for detecting pathogenic agents. In addition, the current work has provided the large overview in blood compatibility and anti-oxidative potential of prepared CQDs and giving useful information for preparing blood–compatible nanoparticles with enhanced bacterial discrimination ability.

ce:section.ce:para: Pathogenic microflora, mainly E. coli bacteria have received special consideration from researchers in form of monitoring their biological menace for the safety and security of communal wellbeing [1]. E. coli symbolizes the most common contagion pathogen for producing deadly infections in living beings [2]. E. coli is largely found in the intestine of warm-blooded organisms and made them susceptible to serious illness such as meningitis, urinary infections, haemorrhagic colitis and gastroenteritis [3,4]. Therefore, it is imperatively crucial to develop highly responsive bacteria sensing methods with high discriminating power and sensitivity. Till date, numerous methodologies, for instance, PCR, labelling with bio chemicals, objective based immunoassays, usage of antibodies, DNA and receptor proteins have been employed for detection of bacterial species [5,6]. In spite of their efficient response rate towards bacteria, these methods have possessed low value of recognition limit towards bacteria [7]. In addition, the lower permanence value with costly and time consuming routes has limited their widespread usage in bacterial sensing [8]. Thus, there is a critical requirement for the generation of quick and effective techniques for bacterial evaluation.

Out of all the available techniques, fluorescence based methodologies have come forward as an efficient mean for bacterial sensing [9,10]. On the basis of this principle, various types of fluorescent probes, such as conjugated dienes, biotins, polymers, streptavidin have been used so far for the detection of bacteria. Recently, the applications of fluorescent based dyes such as, acridine orange, fluoresceins have been employed for tagging pathogens [11,12]. However, on the other hand, these commercially employed fluorescent tags have generated the overlapped emission peaks during analysis and made the detection difficult for multiple bacterial sensing. In addition, the instability and photo bleaching behaviour of these dyes made them ineffective in bacterial sensing application. On contrarily, fluorescent based nanomaterials have emerged as an effective alternative for sensing pathogens. The advanced optical and luminescence properties of nanomaterials with high photo stability has supported their utilization in bacterial sensing. Out of diverse range of fluorescence nanomaterials, carbon based quantum dots (CQDs) have displayed higher sensitivity towards bacterial sensing. The ease of preparation, higher water solubility, amendable fluorescent properties and higher biocompatibility has enhanced their scope in bacterial sensing [13,14]. As one of the potential member of the fluorescent nanomaterials, CQDs have also been employed in designing novel photodynamic therapeutic agents. The CQDs based fluorescent inks, LED lights, and bioimaging agents have currently grabbing the attention among the researchers [15]. Hence, in the current work, we have used the application of CQDs for effective estimation of different concentrations of bacteria.

Keeping the potential of CQDs in mind, the current work represented the facile synthesis of CQDs from single used plastic waste such as plastic bags, cups and bottles made up of polyethylene, polypropylene and polyethylene terephthalate (PET) based polymers as precursor sources. The developed strategy has not effective in bacteria sensing only but also provided an effective mean to overcome the menace caused by excessive usage of plastic. The current strategy has provided a successful methodology for converting single used plastic materials into useful fluorescent materials and has given an effective opportunity for waste management of plastic. Till date, there are limited reports about the fabrication of CQDs from plastic waste (Table S1, supplementary data) [16–20]. However, the application in bacterial sensing is quite few in number [15]. Therefore, the current work presents a novel insight of CQDs as a prospective substitute to commercially employed fluorescence tags in bacteria sensing with low detection limit down to 108 CFU/mL for E. coli. The ease of preparation by using thermal calcination followed by hydrothermal treatment has further enhanced the scope of the developed strategy for large scale production of CQDs. It is significant to describe here that the suggested methodology is quite simplistic, robust and expandable to large scale with advantages. The formed CQDs have possessed high quantum yield, long term stability and maintained their fluorescent properties over a long phase. The respective biocompatibility appraisals have also been carried out by analyzing the hemolysis testing of CQDs. A combination of anticoagulant and thrombolytic activity of CQDs with anti-oxidative activities from reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation has supported their biocompatibility. The prepared CQDs have also displayed higher sensitive and selective sensing aptitude towards E. coli. The worth of the developed method has further enhanced by utilizing the application of prepared CQDs for the testing of bacteria in real water samples. The current nanoprobe has provided several merits over the available literature such as room temperature processing of experimentation, excitation dependent emission behaviour, recyclability of plastic waste, quick response rate with high sensitivity, and non-requirement of complicated instrumentation for bacterial sensing.

The waste plastic polybags, cups and bottles were obtained from Department of Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh, India. Ascorbic acid and hydrogen peroxide were procured from Sigma Aldrich with 99 % purity. Sodium dihydrogen phosphate monohydrate and disodium hydrogen phosphate heptahydrate were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (99 % purity). Nutrient broth was obtained from Himedia with 99 % purity. Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA) and Human Serum Albumin (HSA) were obtained in lyophilized forms from Himedia (99 % purity). Sodium chloride, potassium chloride, conc. HCl, NaOH pellets and potassium ferricyanide were procured from Fisher Scientific with 99 % purity. Ammonium molybdate, sodium sulphate, iron chloride, silver nitrate, EDTA and trichloroacetic acid were purchased from Sigma Aldrich having 99 % purity. All the chemicals mentioned above were used as received without any further processing. Distilled deionised water was utilized for the preparation of the sample solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of CQDs from single used plastic waste
ce:section.ce:para: In current work, thermal calcination followed by hydrothermal procedure was utilized for the conversion of single used plastic waste into useful CQDs. Three different sources of waste plastic such as plastic polybags, cups and bottles were taken in silica crucibles and allowed to heat in a muffle furnace at a uniform temperature of 300 °C for 2 h. Afterwards, the calcinated samples were added into 15 ml of deionized water and transferred into 25 ml of Teflon reactor stainless-steel autoclave. After hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 5 h, the obtained dispersion of three different types of CQDs was allowed to cool down at room temperature. Subsequently, carbonized solutions were centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 15 min to remove the solid residues. The obtained supernatants were further purified through 0.22 μm filter membrane. After lyophilization, as-obtained powdered form of CQDP, CQDc and CQDb (i.e. C-dots obtained from waste polybags, cups and bottles respectively) were eventually stored for future analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of fluorescent CQDs obtained from single used plastic waste
ce:section.ce:para: The obtained CQDs were analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Panalytical X’Pert Pro) for the crystallographic structure recognition and the crystallite size assessment. The morphology was assessed by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Hitachi H7500, 100 kV). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was obtained by using PerkinElmer (RX1) FTIR spectrometer in the wave number range of 400−4000 cm−1. To check the emission profiles of the as prepared particles, all the fluorescence measurements were acquired from a PerkinElmer LS 55 PL spectrometer. The UV–vis. absorption spectra were recorded with a LABINDIA UV–vis. spectrophotometer. AICIL muffle furnace was utilized for calcinating the samples. Size distribution and polydispersity index were examined via dynamic light scattering instrument from Malvern (ZEN 1690). Ultracentrifugation of CQDs was performed on the Remi R-24 centrifuge. The quantum yield (ɸ) for CQDP, CQDC and CQDB were calculated by employing Rhodamine B as a standard reference standard [21]. The surface charge on obtained CQDs was evaluated by Anton Paar Litesizer 500 instrument. For the measurement, suitable amount of formed nanoparticles were mixed with doubly distilled water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQDs biocompatibility appraisals using in-vitro blood studies

ce:section.ce:para: For real sample assay, human blood was collected from normal healthy volunteer, with informed consent. The fresh blood was stored in ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) vacutainer tubes and stored in refrigerator for further analysis.

The blood compatibility of prepared CQDs is another significant standard for investigating their safety for assisting their secure usage in the environmental remediation activities. Herein, hemolysis assay was performed to evaluate the blood compatibility of CQDs. Briefly discussing, blood samples were mixed with 2 mL EDTA solution. The dilution of blood samples was carried out by using 0.9 % saline solution. Subsequently, 0.2 ml of diluted blood solution was added to the known concentrations of CQDs solutions and each sample was further left for equilibration at 37 °C. The obtained suspensions were centrifuged at 5500 rpm for 5 min. Afterwards, the optical absorption measurements of supernatant at 542 nm was carried out for estimating the released hemoglobin from the samples [22]. The reproducibility of the measurements was further verified by executing the measurements twice during the experimentation. The hemolysis ratio was further calculated by using the following equation.
(1)
Hemolysis
%
=
A
s a m p l e
-
A
n e g a t i v e
A
p o s i t i v e
-
A
n e g a t i v e
× 100
Herein, Asample is the absorbance value of the sample (supernatant) which contains blood solution. Apositive and Anegative are the absorbance value of the positive control which contains 0.2 ml diluted blood in 10 ml deionized water and negative control contained 0.2 ml diluted blood in 10 ml saline solution respectively.

Blood clotting assay is another necessary parameter to be scrutinized for CQDs for testing their biocompatible nature towards blood. To examine, 0.24 ml of 0.2 M CaCl2 solution was mixed with 3 ml fresh blood containing EDTA solution. The respective solution was further mixed with different amounts of CQDs ranging from 2 μg to 12 μg and incubated at 37 °C for 10 min. After that, 10 ml of deionised water was added in respective solution and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 1 min. The resultant supernatant was further diluted with 40 ml of deionized water and set to incubate at 37 °C for 1 h [23]. The control blank solution was prepared with deionized water. The value of absorbance for each sample was determined at 542 nm. The blood clotting index was computed by using given equation.
(2)
Blood
clotting
index =
A sample
A blood
× 100
Where Asample is the optical density value of the CQDs solution prepared with blood and Ablood is the absorbance value of the fresh blood solution diluted with deionized water in the absence of CQDs.

The anticoagulant activities of CQDs were evaluated by intermixing of 0.5 ml of 150 mg/mL of different types of CQDs with 1 ml of fresh blood. The respective solution was held at ambient condition (37 °C) to see the visual changes in blood for the coagulation. 0.5 ml of blood sample combined with 0.5 ml of EDTA was set up for positive control. However, 1 ml of fresh collected blood sample was taken as negative control [24].

The thrombolytic activities of CQDs were tested in accordance to the available method by Harish et al. [25 ]. For the analysis, fresh blood clot was spread on grease-free, clean slide. To the samples, 0.3 ml of as synthesized CQDs was added. The visual changes for the dissolution of clot were examined. The untreated blood clot and respective blood clot treated with AgNO3 solution were served as control during the measurements [26].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro stability assay for CQDs over blood components
ce:section.ce:para: Blood being one of the main components of living things, therefore it has been scrutinized as an established standard for investigating the stability of prepared CQDs in current work. The stability was assessed by giving exposure of prepared CQDs over different types of components in blood such as 0.2 M histidine, 0.2 M cysteine, 2% bovine serum albumin (BSA), 2% human serum albumin (HSA), 0.9 % sodium chloride (NaCl), and phosphate buffer with pH ranging from 5.7, 6.2, 7.0 and 7.5. In this study, three different concentrations of prepared CQDs (500, 1000 and 3000 ppm) were scrutinized and deionised water was taken as standard reference solution [27]. All the prepared CQDs solutions were incubated for 48 h separately with the blood components mentioned above. After the incubation, prepared samples were allowed to stand at room temperature for 15 min and their respective absorbance variations were tested for the stability analysis.

The total antioxidant activities of CQDs were checked for detecting their oxidation inhibiting abilities. The measurements give the direct evidence for the biocompatible nature of CQDs. The antioxidant studies gave the description about the free radicals propagation in the biological systems. Therefore, investigation of antioxidant effect of prepared CQDs before any environmental exposure was ensured via carrying out the total antioxidant activities. For the analysis, total antioxidant capacity (TAC) reagent was prepared by mixing 0.6 M H2SO4 with 4 mM ammonium molybdate and 28 mM sodium sulphate solutions. Afterwards, the three types of the prepared CQDs with different concentrations (500, 800 and 1000 ppm) were mixed with methanol solution and then added to 3 ml of TAC reagent. The resultant mixture was held for incubation at 95 °C for 90 min. The measurement of blank standard sample was also performed with deionized water [28,29]. The absorbance of each sample in presence of CQDs was measured at 680 nm after cooling at room temperature. The respective ascorbic acid was used as the positive control. In addition, the ability of CQDs to scavenge hydrogen peroxide was measured by using hydrogen peroxide assay. Briefly explaining, 10 mM solution of hydrogen peroxide was prepared in phosphate buffered saline (PBS) with pH 7.4. The respective 1 ml of the extract containing different concentration of CQDs (500, 800 and 1000 ppm) were rapidly mixed with 2 ml of H2O2 in PBS. The resultant mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 10 min [30]. The absorbance of respective samples was recorded against a blank (without hydrogen peroxide). The percentage of hydrogen peroxide scavenging was determined using the following equation.
(3)
H 2
O 2
scavenging
percentage
=
A o
-
A c
A o
× 100
Where, Ao and Ac are the absorbance values for the control and samples respectively.

In addition, the reducing power of the plastic waste derived CQDs were measured for their electron donor and acceptor potential. These reducing ability measurements of CQDs act as a prominent indicator of their inherent antioxidant ability. For the measurements, three different concentrations of CQDs (2−8 μg/mL) were prepared in 1 ml of deionised water. The respective solution was further mixed with 2.5 ml of 0.2 M potassium ferricyanide and 2.5 ml of 1% phosphate buffer (0.2 M and pH = 6.6). The formed solution was incubated at 50 °C for 30 min and 2.5 ml of 10 % trichloroacetic acid was dissolved in the mixture [31]. The resultant solution was further centrifuged at 2000 rpm for 6 min. The supernatant was extracted and mixed with 2.5 ml of deionised water and 0.5 ml of 0.1 % of ferric chloride solution respectively. Next, each of the prepared solutions were allowed to stir for 10 min and subsequently kept at room temperature for 5 min. The optical density of respective samples was measured at 700 nm for the analysis.

The bacterial detection efficiency of prepared CQDs was studied against gram negative (E. coli) bacteria. For the analysis, bacteria culture of E. coli was kept in nutrient broth. In order to remove the broth, E. coli bacteria (1 × 108 cfu mL−1) was washed with PBS buffer having pH 7.0 for twice and diluted to 107-108 cfu mL−1. Typically, 100 μL of each of CQDs solution was respectively poured to the centrifuge tube containing 1000 μl of different concentrations of E. coli suspension. Then, the prepared mixtures were set for incubation at 37 °C for 1 h. Afterwards, each of the bacterial suspension was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 7 min to isolate the conjugate of E. coli with CQDs. Subsequently, the supernatants of the each of the mixture were estimated for fluorescence bacterial detection. The fluorescence intensities were recorded at 310 nm for the purpose of quantification.

The powder form of three different types of CQDs from plastic cups (CQDc), bottles (CQDb) and poly-bags (CQDP) were obtained by the thermal calcinations at 300 °C in absence of air followed by hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 5 h. The thermal calcinations of waste plastic has induced the breakage in cross-linked polymers in plastic waste and converted into non-condensable black colour mixture. It could further convert into CQDs via hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C. The basic idea of the synthetic process was to reuse single use waste plastic as a carbon source for producing highly fluorescent C-dots. The fabrication has the ability to minimize the working cost of raw materials and able to meet the urgent need of eliminating solid plastic from environment. UV–vis. spectroscopy was carried out to investigate the absorption and band gap behaviour of the prepared CQDs in the range of 200−400 nm with fixed cell length of 1.0 cm. Fig. 1 shows the band gap values of prepared CQDs from waste plastic via using the Tauc plot. The obtained Eg values have shown the variation as per the change in the starting precursor source. For instance, CQDc has displayed the minimum Eg value equivalent to 5.01 eV. However, the values for CQDP and CQDb were 5.07 eV and 5.8 eV respectively. These variations in the values are directly associated with the variations in the sizes and shape of nanoparticles [32,33]. The results have further been varied by analyzing the hydrodynamic radii and HRTEM images (Fig S1, Table S2). The outcomes have also been supported by the results of XRD analysis (Fig. S2 and Table S2). This synthetic methodology has provided a direct control over the shape and morphology of particles without using any kind of external stabilizing agent. The inset of Fig. 1ai to Fig. 1aiii has exhibited a broad absorption band between 230−320 nm for all the three types of prepared CQDs viz. (CQDP), (CQDc) and (CQDb) respectively. The existence of this broad band was mainly explained due to the occurrence of n-π*and π- π* transition in CQDs [34]. The outcome has further supported the sensitivity of the starting precursor source on the optical properties of prepared CQDs. The results have further authenticated by carrying out the fluorescence measurement of prepared CQDs as a function of precursor sources (Fig. 1bi to Fig. 1biii). The emission spectra of CQDs have displayed well defined emission peak at 424 nm for CQDP. However, the peak was shifted to 427 nm and 428 nm for CQDb and CQDc respectively (red line). Additionally, the fluorescence quantum yield (ɸ) values for CQDs were found to be 60 %, 65 % and 69 % for CQDP, CQDc and CQDb respectively.

The obtained value of stock shift in emission spectra was around 90 nm for CQDP, 78 nm and 126 nm for CQDc and CQDb respectively. These changes in stock shift values were associated with the nature of the surface states on CQDs [35]. In addition, the changes in the shape of peaks were also related with the changes in the surface oxidation states in formed CQDs and produced significant effect on the electronic structure of prepared CQDs and made them effective material for bio-sensing. The respective changes in the emission states have further been verified by fitting the Gaussian peaks. The de-convoluted peaks have displayed three sub divided peaks in all the three kind of chosen CQDs (Fig. 1). These split peaks were mainly associated with the three major kind of surface defects in formed CQDs [36]. These respective changes were further explained due to the presence of surface functional groups over the surface of CQDs (Fig. S3). The FT-IR spectrum was recorded for CQDP, CQDc and CQDb to analyse the functional groups and state of prepared particles (Fig. S3). For CQDP, the peak at 2892 cm−1 may represent the C–H bond in the pyrolyzed material. The characteristic peak at 1436 cm−1 confirms the −CH3 group, −CH2 group at 1630 cm−1 and COC bond at 1090 cm-1. The peak at 1630 cm−1 was appeared in the as formed CQDs because of the graphitic nature of formed particles. The broad band for −OH peak of carboxylic acid was observed at 3398 cm-1. The peak for CC bond was appeared at 708 cm-1. In case of CQDc, the sharp peak at 1436 cm−1 affirms the presence of −CH3 group and peak around 1034 cm-1 corresponds to the COC linkage in resultant C-dots. The characteristic peak at 3680 cm-1 appeared due to presence of −OH group in CQDc. The small peak at 916 cm−1 can be attributed to the epoxy ring in the synthesized CQDc. For CQDb, the characteristic peaks observed at 1630 cm-1 and 1882 cm-1 attributed to the CC bond suggesting the graphitic nature of CQDs. The peak appeared at 1406 cm−1 suggests the presence of CH3 group. Next, the peak at 1050 cm−1 confirms the COC glycosidic linkage of cellulose moieties and the peak at 2878 cm−1 can be attributed to the C–H bond stretching vibrations. Also, the broad band at 3414 cm-1 can be due to the −OH group of the carboxylic acid group. The high antioxidant activities of prepared CQDs can be attributed to the presence of hydroxyl groups over their surface. The FT-IR results provide evidence that hydroxyl and carboxyl groups fully adhered over the surface of synthesized CQDs which can play the major role in labelling of bacteria. These comparative results are quite helpful in describing the fluorescence sensing application of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility appraisal of CQDs by using in-vitro blood studies
Hemolysis, blood clotting, thrombolytic activity and anticoagulant assay
ce:section.ce:para: The hemolysis studies in relation to blood for CQDP, CQDc, and CQDb has been carried out to investigate the biocompatibility behaviour of prepared particles from the upcycled plastic waste.The estimation of released hemoglobin was done by measuring the absorbance values of chosen nanoparticle- Red blood cells (RBCs) mixture at 542 nm as a function of different concentrations of CQDs (Fig. 2 a). From the data, it was monitored that the hemolysis provoked by the chosen nanoparticles were not constant and changes with type of chosen CQDs. The hemolysis rate was also displayed the concentration dependency for chosen CQDs. For instance, with 40 ppm of all the types of prepared CQDs has displayed higher hemolysis rate as compared to the lower concentration. However, with maximum concentration of 40 ppm, the hemolytic degree for all three types of CQDs in the tested concentration range was less than 9 % and supported the results that prepared CQDs have satisfied the safety standards of nanoparticles. On further comparing the data, we analyzed that the hemolysis rate of CQDc demonstrates the maximum rate of hemolysis followed by CQDP. However, the lowest hemolysis rate was found for the CQDb nanoparticles. These lower rates of hemolysis have further been explained due to the existence of different types of functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl and carboxylic group over the surface of CQDs. The outcomes of the studies have supported that the prepared CQDs were biocompatible in nature and safe to be utilized when they flowed into blood samples with concentration as high as 40 ppm. In addition, the in-vivo potency of prepared nanoparticles was further judged by carrying out their biocompatibility studies toward blood clotting. The results of blood clotting assay have provided a paramount significance towards the usage of prepared CQDs in biomedical applications where direct contact with blood is required (Fig. 2b).

From the results, it was observed that the blood clotting index value for chosen nanoparticles was quite high and changes with type of chosen CQDs. The blood clotting index has also displayed the concentration dependency for chosen CQDs. With 40 ppm of all the types of prepared CQDs has shown higher clotting index as compared to the lower concentrations. In addition, the high blood clotting index values in presence of CQDs have also supported the results that the chosen nanoparticles have not rupture the blood clotting mechanism at high concentrations. Furthermore, the prepared CQDs have also prevented the formation of blood clots in presence of all the three types of CQDs (Fig. 2c) as compared to the positive control (blood with EDTA). Additionally, the prepared CQDs have also displayed the higher thrombolytic activity by dissolving the blood clot immediately over the surface of the slides (Fig. 2d) as compared to control (coagulated blood). The higher thrombolytic activity of prepared CQDs have supported the potential of prepared nanoparticles in maintaining steady blood flow and in turn supported their future prospective in biomedicines for effectively managing blood disorders.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro stability assay for CQDs over blood components
ce:section.ce:para: In order to better visualize the interaction of prepared CQDs with biological materials, it is prime requisite criteria to perform in vitro stability assay with common blood protein components. For the current assay, high concentrations of our prepared CQDs ranging from 500 ppm to 3000 ppm were exposed to various essential biological components (i.e. histidine, cysteine, bovine serum albumin (BSA), human serum albumin (HSA), sodium chloride (NaCl), and phosphate buffer having pH 5.7, 6.2, 7.0 and 7.5). The optical density for each component is evaluated for carrying out the studies. However, all the three types of prepared CQDs have not displayed any visual changes of aggregation with blood components. This outcome has supported the high stability of prepared CQDs in presence of biological media. In addition, wavelength variation of around 14 nm was found to be in permissible limits and further supported the stability of prepared nanoparticles in presence of biological components (Fig. 3 ). The outcomes of this study has further verified the biocompatibility of the formed nanoparticles and enhanced their applications in biomolecules sensing [37].

The antioxidant capacity for CQDs was also investigated to detect their initial potential of usage in biological molecules. Herein, a diverse range of concentrations ranging from 500 ppm to 1000 ppm for CQDP, CQDc, and CQDb were used for the analysis. From the data, it was found that with increase in the concentration of as prepared CQDs from 500 ppm to 1000 ppm has displayed a slight decrease in the antioxidant abilities (Fig. 4
a to 4c). These prepared CQDs have the proclivity to neutralize the free radical species via the donation of electrons. This was mainly achieved by the existence of hydroxyl groups on their exterior surface (Fig. S3). However, on comparing the three types of prepared CQDs, it was observed that the antioxidant activity of CQDc was slightly higher as compared to CQDP and CQDb. This may be associated with the variations in the size of the formed nanoparticles. The free radical scavenging activity of formed CQDs has also been measured by estimating the absorbance value at 290 nm. From the data, it was clear that the increasing concentrations of CQDs have displayed decrement in the percentage scavenging activity (Fig. 4 d–f). However, the presence of sufficient amount of hydroxyl functional groups over the surface of CQDs has prevented the oxidative degradation of the sample mixture. Thus, these current results have supported the hydrogen peroxide free radical scavenging activity of synthesized CQDs which further led to their use in various biomedical applications.

The corresponding reduction potential measurements have also been carried out to determine the electron donor and acceptor properties of formed CQDs (Fig. S4). These reducing abilities of formed CQDs have provided a significant indicator for their potential antioxidative capacity. For the analysis, the optical density values of CQDP, CQDc, and CQDb were evaluated against the ascorbic acid (control standard). On interpreting the outcomes, a linear increment in the absorbance value was observed as a function of concentrations of chosen nanoparticles. These results have confirmed the high reduction capability of prepared CQDs (Fig. S4). The high reducing ability of CQDs was explained due to the existence of −OH functional group over the surface which further escalated the breakage and disintegration of weak peroxide bond [38].

For each of the chosen samples, the fluorescence intensity was decreased with increase in the concentration. The quenching in emission intensity was chiefly ascribed to the association of the bacteria with the surface functional groups at the exterior facade of CQDs. The above mentioned observations have further attributed to the fact that surface functional groups on prepared CQDs have the tendency to manifest strong interaction with the FimH protein, which exists on the tip of the fimbriae of E. coli [40]. The differences in the sensitivity of prepared CQDs have been explained due to the differences in the degree of CO stretching vibration. These different conjugation abilities of CQDs with FimH protein on the surface of E. coli bacteria has provided different. In addition, the difference in relative size of developed CQDs has also affected the sensitivity of CQDs towards E. coli. The outcomes have provided a novel method for the detection of bacterial pathogen E. coli by using CQDs prepared from plastic waste. In addition, the enhancement in the bacterial concentration in CQDs samples have affected the radiative recombination rate of photo-generated electrons and holes in formed CQDs and which was mainly responsible for the detection of bacteria [39,3].

The limit of detection (LOD) of developed biosensor was found to be 108 CFU/mL. On comparing the results with the available data, it has been found that the LOD values of CQDs prepared in the current work is quite high [40–43]. For instance, CQDs prepared from papaya source has displayed the LOD value as 9.5 × 104 CFU/mL [40]. However, the quantum yield value was around 19 % as compared 60 %, 65 % and 69 % for CQDP, CQDc and CQDb prepared in the current work. Additionaly, the LOD values were found to be superior then the aptamer based biosensor of CQDs for V. parahaemolyticus and S. typhimurium bacterial strains [41]. The quantitative detection of E. coli bacteria has been further verified by stern-Volmer relationship. The outcomes have depicted the linear correlation of emission intensity as a function of bacterial concentration for all chosen CQDs (Fig. 7 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical sensing ability of CQDs in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the practical viability of the developed biosensor, the standard detection method was applied to real water samples (tap water, lake water, deionized water and rain water). The chosen water samples were spiked with different concentrations of E. coli bacteria and their fluorescence emission intensity was analyzed in the presence of as synthesized CQDs at a excitation wavelength of 310 nm (Fig. 8 ). The fluorescence emission profile of all three types of CQDs has exhibited significant quenching in the presence of real water samples. The average recovery of the spiked samples was found to be 93–98 %. The outcomes have supported the quantification of E. coli bacteria in real water samples by using the prepared CQDs from plastic waste and enhanced their future scope in bacteria sensing via using biocompatible particles.

In summary, waste plastic has been demonstrated to be efficient feeding stock for the fabrication of three different types of CQDs via thermal calcination followed by facile hydrothermal treatment. The developed strategy introduces a simple, inexpensive, toxic solvent-free method with the aim of conversion of environmental hazards into valuable nanomaterials. The fabricated CQDs have higher stability without any requirement of extrinsic template and possessed rich functionalities over surface like −COOH and−OH to enhance their unique optical properties with high quantum yield of 60, 65 and 69 % for CQDP, CQDc and CQDb respectively. Interestingly, the formed CQDs have also exhibited higher biocompatibility and possessed non-toxic effect on blood components investigated via blood clotting assay, hemolysis activity, in vitro stability assay, anticoagulant assay, thrombolytic activity and total antioxidant assay. The as prepared CQDs have also possessed potential to act as novel and sensitive fluorescence based biosensor for E. coli bacteria. In addition, this method offered the easiness, high sensitivity, biocompatibility and detection ability within 2 h for chosen bacteria and provided a future scope in biomedical applications, large-scale bacteria/pathogen detection and bacterial contamination control in food chain and water. Overall, the produced CQDs will be lucrative in nanosystem device fabrication for preparing effective and recyclable biosensor with high sensitivity and portability. The as prepared nanosensor provided easy fabrication with fast response with high sensitivity and selectivity in real water samples which might be necessary for detecting pathogenic agents.

Manisha Kumari: Methodology, Conceptualization, Software, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Savita Chaudhary: Methodology, Conceptualization, Validation, Formal analysis, Resources, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors reported no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Savita Chaudhary is thankful to DST Inspire Faculty award [IFACH- 17], Haryana State Council for Science and Technology [HSCSIT/R&D/2020/476] and DST Purse grants II for financial assistance.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2020.111333.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
